flashcards.front: What is decidability in Theory of Computation?
flashcards.back: A problem is decidable if there exists an algorithm that, given any input to the problem, will always halt and produce the correct yes/no answer in finite time.
The Halting Problem asks: given a program and its input, determine whether the program will eventually halt or run forever. It was proven undecidable by Alan Turing in 1936.
A language L is recursive/decidable if there exists a Turing Machine that halts on every input (accepts strings in L, rejects strings not in L). Both membership and complement are decidable.
A language L is recursively enumerable if there exists a Turing Machine that accepts all strings in L but may either reject or loop forever on strings not in L.

Rice's Theorem states: Any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is undecidable. A property is trivial if it's either empty (no TM has it) or universal (all TMs have it).

flashcards.front: What is a Reduction in Undecidability?
flashcards.back: Reduction transforms one problem (A) into another problem (B). If A is undecidable and we can reduce A to B, then B is also undecidable. This proves undecidability of new problems.

flashcards.front: What is the Post Correspondence Problem (PCP)?
flashcards.back: PCP asks: given pairs of strings, can we arrange them in a sequence where the concatenation of first components equals the concatenation of second components? PCP is undecidable.

The Church-Turing Thesis states that any computation performed by any algorithm can be performed by a Turing Machine. It establishes the equivalence of intuitive computability and Turing computability.
Given a string w and a TM M, does M accept w? This is undecidable for Type-0 (unrestricted) grammars but decidable for context-free grammars (CYK algorithm).

flashcards.front: What is the Emptiness Problem for TMs?
flashcards.back: Given a TM M, does it accept any string at all (L(M) ≠ ∅)? The emptiness problem is undecidable for Turing machines.

flashcards.front: What is the Equivalence Problem for TMs?
flashcards.back: Given two TMs M1 and M2, do they accept the same language (L(M1) = L(M2))? This problem is undecidable.
Given a TM M, is the language L(M) regular? This is undecidable by Rice's Theorem (regularity is a non-trivial property of languages).

flashcards.front: Differentiate between Decidable and Undecidable problems
flashcards.back: Decidable problems have an algorithm that always halts with correct answer. Undecidable problems have no such algorithm - either no algorithm exists or any algorithm may fail to halt on some inputs.
A Universal Turing Machine can simulate any other Turing Machine. It takes as input: (description of TM M, input w) and simulates M on w. Used in proving Halting Problem undecidable.

flashcards.front: What is a trivial property in Rice's Theorem context?
flashcards.back: A property is trivial in Rice's Theorem if either: (1) No TM has the property (empty set), or (2) All TMs have the property (universal set). Only non-trivial properties are undecidable.
